title: Ranunculus robustifrons
taxonomy: Ranunculus robustifrons is a species of flowering plant in the family Ranunculaceae. It belongs to the genus Ranunculus, which consists of approximately 600 species of buttercups and their relatives. Ranunculus robustifrons is native to China and is a perennial plant that grows in alpine meadows and rocky areas. The plant has yellow flowers and is a member of the Ranunculales order, which includes many aquatic plants. Ranunculus robustifrons is an alpine species that thrives in areas with high elevations and well-drained soils.
uses: Ranunculus robustifrons is a plant native to Australia, and it has been used in traditional medicine. The plant's leaves and stems are reported to have antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties. Ranunculus robustifrons is used to treat a variety of conditions, including skin irritations and infections. In some cases, it has also been used to treat respiratory issues, such as coughs and colds. More research is needed to confirm the plant's full range of medicinal uses and potential side effects.

side_effects: Ranunculus robustifrons, a plant native to New Zealand, contains toxins such as ranunculin and protoanemonin, which can cause skin irritation and other side effects when ingested or touched. Ingestion of the plant can lead to oral and gastrointestinal irritation, and may cause vomiting and diarrhea. Skin contact with the plant can result in redness, itching, and blistering. Prolonged exposure to the plant's toxins can cause more severe symptoms, including kidney damage and other systemic effects. It is essential to exercise caution when handling Ranunculus robustifrons to avoid exposure to its toxic compounds.
